title: Feature Misspecification in Sequential Learning Problems
abstract: We consider a class of sequential learning problems where a decision maker must learn the unknown statistical characteristics of a finite set of alternatives (or systems) using sequential sampling to ultimately select a subset of ‚Äúgood‚ÄĚ alternatives. A salient feature of our problem is that system performance is governed by a set offeatures. The decision maker postulates the dependence on these features to be linear, but this model may not precisely represent the true underlying system structure. We show that this misspecification, if not managed properly, can lead to suboptimal performance because of a phenomenon identified assample-selection endogeneity. We propose aprospective samplingprinciple‚ÄĒa new approach that eliminates the adverse effects of misspecification as the number of samples grows large. The proposed principle applies across a very general class of widely used sampling policies, enjoys strong asymptotic performance guarantees, and exhibits effective finite-sample performance in numerical experiments.

author.Dohyun Ahn: Department of Systems Engineering and Engineering Management, The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Sha Tin, New Territories, Hong Kong
author.Dongwook Shin: HKUST Business School, Clear Water Bay, Kowloon, Hong Kong
author.Assaf Zeevi: Graduate School of Business, Columbia University, New York, New York 10025

article.1. Introduction: In most operations problems, prescriptive solutions are usually predicated on the specification of a model for the underlying system of interest. A critical yet common question in this context is whether the postulated model ismisspecifiedwith respect to the true system characteristics. This problem is well studied in the statistics and econometrics literature with a focus on inference, and several estimation methods are known to possess desirable statistical properties even in the presence of model misspecification; seeWhite (1996)for a broad overview of this agenda. (Further discussion of related literature is deferred toSection 2.) These statistical guarantees, however, do not naturally translate into many problems in the field of operations research and management science, where decisions are typically derived from optimization of an objective function that is explicitly built on the postulated (and possibly misspecified) statistical model. These issues are especially problematic insequentiallearning problems, where a postulated model is typically first estimated and then used to make decisions, which are then, in turn, used to collect more data and update the estimates of the model. In this process, the interplay between the data-collection mechanism and optimization-based decisions may intensify the (negative) effect of model misspecification. In this paper, we seek to improve our understanding of the impact of model misspecification in the context of prototypical sequential decision-making problems that we describe next. 1.1. High-Level Overview of the Problem A decision maker faces a finite number of stochastic alternatives, which will be referred to as ‚Äúsystems,‚ÄĚ where the statistical characteristics of random payoffs generated by the systems are initially unknown but can be learned via sampling. Given a fixed and finite sampling budget, the ultimate goal is to identify top-ranked systems in terms of their expected payoffs. This is broadly referred to as an ordinal optimization problem; seeSection 2for a detailed review of ordinal optimization problems. A common objective in such problems is to allocate samples across the systems to maximize the likelihood of correctly identifying the top-ranked systems. The following are representative examples. (Generalized A/B testing) A typical example arises in product testing, where different versions of a product are offered to a small subset of customers to ultimately find the most successful version for the entire customer base (Kohavi et al. 2009). Because it is typically expensive to access customers‚Äô feedback, effective experimentation can help efficiently identify the best version given a fixed number of customers. (Simulation optimization) A decision maker wishes to select the best of several policies applied to a stochastic Markov decision process. The policies may be evaluated only through Monte Carlo simulation, and sampling efforts are efficiently allocated to identify the best policy in the shortest amount of time. A simple approach to solving such problems is to allocate the sampling budgetequallyacross the systems, but such a sampling strategy may waste too many samples on systems that are far from optimal. Alternatively, effective sampling strategies must adaptively allocate sampling efforts while learning through sample observations. With few exceptions, most prior work focuses on sampling strategies that combine myopic optimization and sequential estimation as follows: when a sampling decision is made, the decision maker first estimates the unknown probability distributions of the systems and then allocates samples to maximize a certaininformation metricas if the aforementioned estimates are accurate. Examples include expected improvement methods (Frazier et al. 2008,Chick et al. 2010,Salemi et al. 2014), posterior sampling methods (Agrawal and Goyal 2012,Russo 2020), optimal computing budget allocation (OCBA) methods (Chen et al. 2000,2008), and large deviations‚Äďbased sampling methods (Glynn and Juneja 2004,Shin et al. 2018). These methods are designed to achieve strong performance guarantees forwell-specifiedmodels; however, as we will demonstrate, they may perform poorly when the model ismisspecified. 1.2. Feature Misspecification In traditional settings, a probability distribution is associated with each system, and thus, the payoff from a given system does not inform the statistical characteristics of the other systems. Therefore, any (dynamic) sampling strategy becomes inefficient as the number of competing systems grows large relative to a finite sampling budget (Branke et al. 2007). A possible remedy is to describe each system viafeaturesthat contain detailed information about its statistical characteristics. The system model is then specified as a parametric structure that describes the relationship between the features and the expected payoffs. For example, owing to the ever-increasing number of items (products or services) in online marketplaces, it has become common practice to build principal features that provide a parsimonious description of the item (e.g., color/size of shirts, artist/genre of movies); seeLu et al. (2015)for a comprehensive survey in the context of recommendation engines. As stated, this feature-based approach is an effective and widely used way to reduce dimensionality and handle a large number of systems. At the same time, it is rarely true that the expected payoffs of the systems are perfectly represented by the available features and the parametric model that harnesses them. In this paper, we consider model misspecification caused by an imperfect feature representation; for example, the model could be missing relevant features and/or some interaction between features that are determinants of the payoffs.1 In statistical modeling, a common approach used to mitigate misspecification is to grow the model complexity as further data become available (this is part and parcel of nonparametric statistics). However, this mitigation clearly comes at the expense of computation and further obstacles in deriving tractable policies, which is especially detrimental in operations and decision-making contexts. The focus of our paper takes it as given that the true model is not within our grasp, and it aims to better understand whether, how, and to what extent misspecification impacts inefficiencies in dynamic sampling methods. 1.3. Main Focus of the Paper When a feature-based model is misspecified, one source of inefficiency stems from the fact that the estimated parameters may be biased, depending on how samples are allocated across the systems throughout the learning process. This phenomenon, henceforth referred to assample-selection endogeneity, is illustrated inFigure 1. In misspecified settings, the true model (denoted by ‚äó) lies outside of the oval region, which represents the span of the feature-based parametric model. In other words, any feature-based model in this class entails some bias in the expected payoffs of the systems. The primary focus of our work is to find a parametric model that, despite being biased, is the most effective in terms of the goal of ranking systems. As will be formalized later inSection 5, without explicit efforts to mitigate misspecification effects, the extant design principles may constantly converge to spurious operating points (denoted by‚ÄĘ) rather than the one that attains optimal performance (denoted by ‚Äú‚Ė†‚ÄĚ). Based on these insights, our objective is to propose a ‚Äúdesign principle‚ÄĚ to construct dynamic sampling policies that mitigate the negative effect of model misspecification, thereby achieving the best performance within the assumed model and its limitations. 1.4. Summary of the Main Results We first consider a very general class of myopic sampling strategies as benchmarks. These include various methods commonly explored in the extant literature, where sampling decisions are made by maximizing an information metric that typically does not take into account the possibility of model misspecification. We demonstrate through several examples that such sampling strategies suffer from performance loss in misspecified settings, and we propose a new design principle to effectively counter that. In detail, the paper contributes along the three dimensions discussed below. 1.4.1. Quantifying Performance Loss Because of Misspecification. In statistics and econometrics, the effect of misspecification is often measured in terms of the degree of fit to underlying data. In this paper, we quantify the effect of misspecification from an operational perspective; instead of measuring the degradedfitto underlying data, we measure the degradedperformancebecause of misspecification. In particular, when a model is misspecified, it is possible that the observed data appear ‚Äúconsistent‚ÄĚ with the misspecified model, yet striving to improve parameter estimates does not necessarily result in maximizing the likelihood of identifying the correct ranking of the systems. 1.4.2. Prospective Maximum-Likelihood (ML) Estimators. It is well-known that the maximum-likelihood estimator possesses desirable statistical properties even in the presence of misspecification (White 1982). Thus, one may intuit that this estimator is also reasonable to use in the parametric (feature-based) ordinal optimization problem. Contrary to this intuition, we find that a vanilla ML estimator leads to performance loss; specifically, sampling decisions based on this estimator place ‚Äútoo much emphasis‚ÄĚ on minimizing a statistical distance from the true model and ‚Äúnot enough‚ÄĚ emphasis on the goal of ranking systems, leading to a suboptimal outcome (for example, the point ‚Äú‚Ė≤‚ÄĚ inFigure 1). To address this, we propose a new design principle, prospective sampling (PROS), that is designed to address the aforementioned problem because of model misspecification. PROS uses aweightedML estimation, where the weights are judiciously prescribed to counter the sample-selection endogeneity. Somewhat surprisingly, we show that such a simple modification is sufficient to alleviate the effect of model misspecification in the context of our feature-based problem. 1.4.3. Asymptotic Efficiency Under the PROS Principle. Our analysis reveals that as the sampling budget grows large, PROS cannot only identify the correct ranking of the systems but also achieve maximum efficiency in terms of postulated information metrics. We benchmark the performance of PROS through simulation studies and find that existing sampling strategies‚ÄĒwhich do not take into account misspecification‚ÄĒcan be very ineffective and possibly fail to identify the correct ranking, even asymptotically as the sampling budget grows large. In particular, even in Gaussian settings where the OCBA strategies are known to be efficient (Chen et al. 2000,2008), a PROS version of OCBA outperforms plain OCBA if the parametric structure of the systems is misspecified. It is worth noting that the problem formulation we work with, which is presented inSection 3, encompasses a broad array of myopic sampling strategies in the ordinal optimization/best-arm identification literature. Because PROS and our analysis accommodate this level of generality, they can be specialized to problems that, in the past, have been studied in isolation. Our main analysis focuses on the linear Gaussian model, which best elucidates the key insights and characteristics of the PROS principle by making the analysis more transparent. InSection 7, we show that our design principle extends to the generalized linear model (GLM) (McCullagh and Nelder 1989). Before closing the section, we note that our model is related but distinct from standard contextual bandit models, where side information, or context, is given in each time step and the payoffs of the systems depend both on this side information as well as the index of the system; seeSection 2for a broad overview. In our model, side information is given asfixedsystem-specific features that are part of the structure of systems‚Äô payoffs. 1.5. Organization of the Paper The remainder of the paper is organized as follows. InSection 2, we survey some related literature. InSection 3, we introduce our modeling framework based on linear Gaussian models, formalize the notion of model misspecification, and illustrate its effect on parametric inference. InSection 4, we present illustrative examples of sequential learning problems. InSection 5, we analyze the performance of sampling policies that ignore model misspecification. InSection 6, we propose our design principle and characterize its asymptotic performance. InSection 7, we extend our analysis under linear Gaussian models to general cases of GLM. InSection 8, we compare PROS with benchmark policies via simulation studies. All proofs of our theoretical results are relegated to the Online Appendix.
article.2. Literature Review: In this section, for space considerations, we review work that is most relevant to our current paper; readers are referred to Section EC.1 of the Online Appendix for a more comprehensive review. 2.1. Model Misspecification In classical statistics and econometrics, model misspecification has been widely studied, particularly focusing on parametric estimators and their properties such as consistency and asymptotic normality; cf.White (1996)for an overview of this topic. One approach to mitigate misspecification risk is nonparametric modeling, where the model complexity is not specified a priori; as more data become available, model complexity is allowed to increase and ultimately can eliminate model misspecification. In the context of operations research and management science (OR/MS), several works take advantage of the nonparametric approach to address misspecification in demand modeling (Besbes and Zeevi 2009,Wang et al. 2014,Chen et al. 2019), consumer choice models (Rusmevichientong et al. 2006,van Ryzin and Vulcano 2015), and various other applications. As alluded to earlier, our approach is quite different: instead of striving to eliminate model misspecification, we study (possibly misspecified) parametric models that facilitate tractability and efficiency in online decision making, and we propose ways to counter the potential negative effects of model misspecification without the need to increase model complexity. 2.2. Sequential Learning Problems In most sequential learning problems, the main objective is to develop (near-)optimal algorithms that judiciously balance the tension between exploration (learning) and exploitation (optimizing). One popular example is the multiarmed bandit (MAB) problem, where the goal is to maximize the cumulative rewards or payoffs from a set of arms (or systems) whose probability distributions (or other key statistics) are initially unknown.2In this strand of literature, contextual bandit problems, where the payoff depends on the system index as well as on side information (or covariates) that is realized at each time step, have recently received much attention; see, for example,Agrawal (2019)and references therein. Such contextual settings have also been adopted in the ordinal optimization literature (Shen et al. 2017,Li et al. 2018). Our setup, which builds on known (and fixed) features associated with each system, superficially resembles the contextual settings mentioned above, but the two have different applications. For example, in e-commerce markets, platforms can employ contextual approaches to take advantage of customer-specific information (captured by covariates) and make (user-based) personalized recommendations, whereas platforms may use feature-based approaches to encode products (systems) and provide common (item-based) recommendations to their customers.3The feature-based approach we consider can also be used for product testing and selection, where fixed product features (as part of the payoff structure) are at least partially known. Our work elucidates feature misspecification effects and hence does not involve time-instant by time-instant randomly generated side information, which is characteristic of contextual paradigms. In practical applications, many data-driven optimization problems employestimate-then-optimizemethods, where one first infers unknown parameters using statistical models and then makes decisions by solving an optimization problem, assuming that the unknown parameters are the most recent estimates.4Several studies demonstrate that if estimate-then-optimize methods are employed in a greedy fashion to solve sequential learning problems, the parameter estimates may not be consistent, a phenomenon that is commonly referred to asincomplete learning; see, for example,Taylor (1974),Lai and Robbins (1982),Keskin and Zeevi (2018), andden Boer and Zwart (2014)in a more specialized pricing problem. All the aforementioned works concern well-specified models. In this paper, we study the estimate-then-optimize approach when the estimation model is allowed to be misspecified, which introduces new challenges in sequential learning problems, as we discuss next. 2.3. Misspecification in Sequential Learning Problems In the MAB literature, a prototypical class of sequential learning problems, many algorithms exhibit strong performance guarantees under well-specified models, but recent papers characterize the increased complexity because of model misspecification (Ghosh et al. 2017,Foster et al. 2019,Foster et al. 2020,Lattimore et al. 2020,Pacchiano et al. 2020). Closer to the operations research/management science (OR/MS) field, several studies in the revenue management area point out the negative potential of misspecification, which can be further amplified by optimization-based decisions embedded in sequential learning procedures, for example, capacity booking (Cooper et al. 2006), oligopoly pricing (Cooper et al. 2015), and pricing with contextual information (Nambiar et al. 2019). Of these, our paper is closest toNambiar et al. (2019). Specifically, their paper focuses on dynamic pricing problems with contextual information and shows that model misspecification can lead to an endogeneity issue‚ÄĒa correlation between price and demand estimation error‚ÄĒwhich, in turn, leads to suboptimal pricing decisions.Nambiar et al. (2019)also propose a random price shock algorithm that is robust to model misspecification. In contrast, our paper concernssample-selection endogeneityin sequential allocation problems under model misspecification and shows that a simple regularization of the estimation loss function can counter the negative effects of misspecification. A similar approach has been studied in static learning environments (see, e.g.,Kao et al. 2009,Elmachtoub and Grigas 2021), where estimation loss functions are taken to incorporate the structure of the optimization problem. In particular,Elmachtoub and Grigas (2021)show via numerical testing that the proposed method exhibits promising performance in misspecified settings. 2.4. Sequential Learning in Ordinal Optimization As alluded to earlier, the broad objective of this paper is to investigate the consequences of model misspecification in sequential learning problems. To best illustrate the key ideas, we focus on prototypical ordinal optimization problems that involve an (irrevocable) selection decision at the end of the sampling horizon (e.g., selecting which version of a product to commercialize after testing). In traditional settings of ordinal optimization without features, the system provides observations (samples) independent of all others, and the goal is to maximize the probability of selecting the system with the highest mean, given a finite sampling budget. A pioneering work byChen et al. (2000)suggests a tractable sampling policy called optimal computing budget allocation; seeChen and Lee (2011)for an overview of related works. In this body of work, each system isassumedto have Gaussian payoffs with known variance. Although this assumption drastically simplifies analysis, it may not be true in practice, andGlynn and Juneja (2004)show that the standard argument based on the central limit theorem cannot validate sampling procedures derived from a misspecified Gaussian model. Nonetheless,Shin et al. (2018)show that the performance loss because of the misspecified Gaussian model is not detrimental if the difference in means between the best and second-best systems is suitably ‚Äúsmall.‚ÄĚ Unlike these prior works, our paper sheds light on the implications of model misspecification infeature-basedsettings where further subtleties arise,5and it places further emphasis on mitigation strategies. 2.5. Fixed-Confidence Guarantees in Feature-Based Settings Ordinal optimization shares some common threads with ‚Äúbest-arm identification‚ÄĚ (or ‚Äúpure exploration‚ÄĚ) problems in the MAB literature, where the general goal is to find a probably-approximately-correct (PAC) learning algorithm that ensures optimal system selection at a given confidence level while taking as few samples as possible; seeKaufmann et al. (2016)and references therein. Our feature-based setting is largely motivated by recent works in best-arm identification in parametric (feature-based) bandits. (Relevant studies in traditional settings without features are discussed in Section EC.1 of the Online Appendix.) When the performance of each system is described by a linear combination of known features,Soare et al. (2014)andXu et al. (2018)propose static algorithms and an adaptive one, respectively, and characterize their sample complexities. More recently,Kazerouni and Wein (2021)generalize the results ofXu et al. (2018)to the well-known statistical framework of generalized linear models (McCullagh and Nelder 1989). To the best of our knowledge, none of the aforementioned works in feature-based settings consider model misspecification, and in contrast to the ‚Äúfixed confidence‚ÄĚ framework, the literature is scant on the theoretical investigation of feature-based settings in the ‚Äúfixed budget‚ÄĚ framework to which our model belongs.

article.7. Extensions to the Generalized Linear: In this section, we extend our PROS principle to the generalized linear models (McCullagh and Nelder 1989). In this modeling framework, the expected payoff is assumed to follow a nonlinear function of a linear combination of features, and the underlying probability distributions are not necessarily Gaussian. Thus, the information metrics introduced inExamples 1to4are not directly applicable to the cases of GLM. In what follows, we develop an information metric under the GLM, which is possibly misspecified, and construct sampling policies based on the PROS principle. Although our PROS principle for GLM can be applied in various contexts, in this section, we focus on the best-subset selection problem inExample 1to illustrate the applicability in one of the most archetypal settings. Extensions for other examples can be found in Section EC.2.6 of the Online Appendix. 7.1. Although the PROS policy exhibits a desirable theoretical guarantee on asymptotic performance, the implementation of the policy entails significant computational challenges. In each phase, the evaluation of the information metricŌĀt(őĪ;őłňútŌÄ(őĪ))givenőĪrequires solving the Bilevel Optimization Problem (25) for each pair of systems (i,j),i=1,‚Ä¶,‚Ąď, andj=‚Ąď+1,‚Ä¶,k. This paper does not address these computational challenges: our focus is restricted to elucidating abstract design principles that overcome the negative effects of model misspecification.
article.8. Numerical Experiments: In this section, we present simulation studies to validate the efficacy of our proposed design principle. The focus is not on comparing different information metrics. Although this could be important from a practical standpoint, a fair comparison between information metrics is challenging because they are derived under different modeling assumptions and objectives, and their performance depends on the choice of tuning parameters. Our aim is to compare the performance of the oblivious and PROS policies for a given information metric in a wide range of problem settings. 8.1. Experimental Settings In our numerical study, we consider four problem settings. In the first three settings, we consider the best-subset selection problems with information metrics taken fromExamples 1to3, which will be referred to as large deviations (LD), OCBA, and GapE, respectively. In the last setting, we consider the feasibility determination problem with the information metric taken fromExample 4. In all settings, we estimate the performance measures by taking the average ofN=105simulation trials. This guarantees that the standard errors of the estimated performance measures are at least an order of magnitude smaller than the estimates in our experiments. Additionally, tuning parameters are set asp0=500andp=‚ÄČ300. 8.1.1. Best-Subset Selection: Linear Gaussian Models. We consider eight systems whose probability distributions are linear Gaussian with known standard deviationŌÉi=7fori=1,‚Ä¶,8. The mean vector and the feature matrix for the eight systems are given by 8.1.2. Best-Subset Selection: Logit-Bernoulli Models. We next consider six systems whose probability distributions are characterized by logit-Bernoulli models, where the density functions are characterized as (22) withhi(yi)=1,ŌĄi=1, andA(z)=log(1+exp(z)). The mean vector and the feature matrix for the six systems are given by Similar to the case of linear Gaussian models, we consider two system configurations,őě1‚Ä≤andőě2‚Ä≤: the first configurationőě1‚Ä≤is correctly specified by the full feature matrix (d=‚ÄČ6), whereas the secondőě2‚Ä≤is misspecified by a partial feature matrix that consists only of the first three features (d=‚ÄČ3). 8.1.3. Best-Subset Selection: Log-Poisson Models. We also consider six systems whose probability distributions are characterized by log-Poisson models, where the density functions are characterized as (22) withhi(yi)=1/(yi!),ŌĄi=1, andA(z)=exp(z). The mean vector and the feature matrix for the six systems are given by Similar to the case of logit-Bernoulli models, we consider two system configurations,őě1‚Ä≥andőě2‚Ä≥: the first configurationőě1‚Ä≥is correctly specified by the full feature matrix (d=‚ÄČ6), whereas the secondőě2‚Ä≥is misspecified by a partial feature matrix that consists only of the first three features (d=‚ÄČ3). 8.1.4. Feasibility Determination: Linear Gaussian Models. We finally consider the feasibility determination problem inExample 4under the system configurationsőě1andőě2. The feasibility criterion is set asőľmin=0.93for which the first two systems are feasible. The performance of a sampling policy is measured by taking the average of the number of incorrect determinations. 8.2. Summary of the Numerical Results Figures 5‚Äď7illustrate the results of the four problem settings described inSection 8.1. In all numerical experiments, the oblivious policies perform well when the model is correctly specified. In the misspecified settings, however, the parametric model estimated by the oblivious policy leads to a degraded performance or, possibly, a complete failure in minimizing the expected loss; this is the operating point denoted by‚ÄĘinFigure 1. In particular, their performance is even worse than the performance of equal allocation under all misspecified models. In contrast, we find that the PROS policies exhibit robust performance no matter whether the model is misspecified. Recall that the PROS policies asymptotically attain the allocation that leads the corresponding parametric model to achieve the best performance (denoted by ‚Äú‚Ė†‚ÄĚ inFigure 1). Consequently, this results in superior outcomes in all misspecified settings that we study. One can observe that the oblivious policies slightly outperform the PROS policies in the correctly specified setting, but this result should be interpreted with caution because models are misspecified to certain extents in most, if not all, practical applications. In such circumstances, the oblivious policies may fail to show consistent performance, whereas our PROS principle provides a hedge against the risk of misspecification.
